coredata.dc:title: Divergent effects of biomass-derived carbon dots application and sweetpotato planting on accumulations of soil microbial necromass carbon in Vertisol
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jing
Zhu, Meng
Zhu, Xiaoya
Zhang, Qiangqiang
Yu, Yongchao
Zhao, Peng
Liu, Ming
Jin, Rong
Tang, Zhonghou
coredata.dc:description: Biomass-derived nano carbon dots (CDs) application and sweetpotato (SP) planting can alter soil microbial community structure. However, the impact of these treatments on soil microbial necromass carbon (MNC) and associated mechanisms remains unclear. In this study, we combined pot experiments and laboratory analyses to assess soil physicochemical properties, microbial community characteristics, metabolic enzyme activity and multivariate correlations, aiming to explore the determinants of soil MNC accumulation in Vertisol following CDs application and SP planting. The results showed inconsistent effects of CDs application on soil bacterial and fungal necromass C content. Although CDs application increased bacterial richness and the relative abundances of Proteobacteria, Firmicutes and Cyanobacteria in the absence of SP planting, it failed to enhance bacterial necromass C accumulation. In contrast, CDs application improved soil fungal necromass C content regardless of SP planting. Besides directly increasing soil organic carbon (SOC) concentrations, both CDs application and SP planting enhanced the contributions of fungal necromass C to SOC. However, SP planting neither increased soil dissolved organic carbon (DOC) nor altered the compound contents in DOC solution. Extracellular enzymes related to C-cycling (e.g., β-α-cellobiohydrolase and β-1,4-xylosidase) also significantly diminished under SP planting without CDs application. Linear discriminant analysis (LDA) identified distinct bacterial and fungal genera between the CDs application and SP planting treatments. Structural equation models (SEMs) revealed that the reduced accumulation of bacterial necromass C was primarily driven by increased β-1,4-glucosidase activity and shifted in bacterial community composition, which limited microbial substrate utilization and growth. The increased fungal necromass C accumulation was attributed to altering fungal community structure and decreasing α-diversity, which promoted necromass formation through sequential assimilation, synthesis, and turnover of CDs and SP carbon inputs. These results highlight the differential responses of bacterial and fungal necromass accumulation to CDs application and SP planting, providing novel insights into the regulatory roles of nano CDs and plant-microbe interactions in SOC sequestration processes in Vertisol.

head.ce:title: Divergent effects of biomass-derived carbon dots application and sweetpotato planting on accumulations of soil microbial necromass carbon in Vertisol

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomass-derived nano carbon dots (CDs) application and sweetpotato (SP) planting can alter soil microbial community structure. However, the impact of these treatments on soil microbial necromass carbon (MNC) and associated mechanisms remains unclear. In this study, we combined pot experiments and laboratory analyses to assess soil physicochemical properties, microbial community characteristics, metabolic enzyme activity and multivariate correlations, aiming to explore the determinants of soil MNC accumulation in Vertisol following CDs application and SP planting. The results showed inconsistent effects of CDs application on soil bacterial and fungal necromass C content. Although CDs application increased bacterial richness and the relative abundances of Proteobacteria, Firmicutes and Cyanobacteria in the absence of SP planting, it failed to enhance bacterial necromass C accumulation. In contrast, CDs application improved soil fungal necromass C content regardless of SP planting. Besides directly increasing soil organic carbon (SOC) concentrations, both CDs application and SP planting enhanced the contributions of fungal necromass C to SOC. However, SP planting neither increased soil dissolved organic carbon (DOC) nor altered the compound contents in DOC solution. Extracellular enzymes related to C-cycling (e.g., β-α-cellobiohydrolase and β-1,4-xylosidase) also significantly diminished under SP planting without CDs application. Linear discriminant analysis (LDA) identified distinct bacterial and fungal genera between the CDs application and SP planting treatments. Structural equation models (SEMs) revealed that the reduced accumulation of bacterial necromass C was primarily driven by increased β-1,4-glucosidase activity and shifted in bacterial community composition, which limited microbial substrate utilization and growth. The increased fungal necromass C accumulation was attributed to altering fungal community structure and decreasing α-diversity, which promoted necromass formation through sequential assimilation, synthesis, and turnover of CDs and SP carbon inputs. These results highlight the differential responses of bacterial and fungal necromass accumulation to CDs application and SP planting, providing novel insights into the regulatory roles of nano CDs and plant-microbe interactions in SOC sequestration processes in Vertisol.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Soil organic carbon plays an important role in carbon cycling of agricultural ecosystems (Liang et al., 2019). Increasing the storage of SOC is of great significance for improving the soil quality in farmland and neutralizing carbon emissions. Decomposed organic materials, plant residues, rhizodeposits and microbial necromass are the main sources for the formation of SOC (Wang et al., 2021a; Cotrufo et al., 2015; Kögel-Knabner and Rumpel, 2018). In recent years, increasing evidence has shown that microbial necromass constitutes a dominant proportion of SOC (Ma et al., 2018; Liang et al., 2019), primarily due to the recalcitrance of their cell wall materials (Liang et al., 2017).

Soil microbial necromass is formed and accumulated after a rapid cycle of cell death and fragmentation period (Cotrufo et al., 2013, 2015; Kallenbach et al., 2016). Luo et al. (2021) reveal the C pathways from rhizodeposits through fungal necromass and organo-mineral associations for the build-up of SOC in paddy fields. Plant residues are assimilated into small molecular weight compounds by microorganisms through the pathways of in vivo turnover or ex vivo modification (Wang et al., 2021b; Camenzind et al., 2022). The effects of farmland management practices (e.g. inorganic fertilizers application, organic amendments, intercropping, and cover crops) on microbial necromass have attracted more and more attention (Zhou et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2023; Hu et al., 2023). For example, chemical fertilizers application accelerated SOC dynamics by altering microbial turnover and necromass accumulation (Ni et al., 2021). Positive (Hu et al., 2022), neutral (Chen et al., 2018), and negative effects (Liang and Balser, 2012) of chemical fertilization on microbial necromass were observed. Manure increased bacterial necromass more than fungal necromass, leading to decreased ratio of fungal-to-bacterial necromass. Greater increases in microbial necromass were common in semi-arid and cool climates, particularly in soils with pH< 8 after straw amendments (Zhou et al., 2023). Crop diversity-driven changes in root traits induced microbial adaptations that promote microbial necromass accumulation (Zhao et al., 2023). Cover crops positively influenced bacterial and fungal necromass C in all SOC fractions, while non cover crops only affected fungal necromass C in bulk soil (Hu et al., 2023). Although traditional farmland management measures have advanced studies on microbial necromass C, reports on necromass accumulation driven by emerging nanocarbon fertilizers remain limited.

Recently, nanomaterials derived from plant biomass or metals have developed rapidly due to their potential to improve SOC and alter soil microbial diversity. Mahmoud et al. (2024) demonstrated that nanobiochar significantly increased soil fertility, organic matter content, and microbial biomass carbon. The biomass of soil bacteria and fungi was shown to be related with the concentration of carbon nanotubes (Jin et al., 2014). Carbon nanomaterials had an effect on alpha and beta diversity of microbial functional genes (Wu et al., 2020). Furthermore, the metabolic activity of soil microbial communities was affected by the exposure scenarios of carbon nanomaterials (Liu et al., 2023). For instance, exposure to ZnO nanoparticles enhances enzymatic activities related to soil C cycle and alters bacterial community structure at the phylum and genus levels.(Shi et al., 2020). Additionally, significant differences were observed in rhizosphere bacterial communities, with Bacteroidota and Actinobacteriota identified as valuable biomarkers for zinc oxide nanoparticles (Chen et al., 2023a, 2023b). Among them, nano carbon dots (CDs) exhibit advantages such as small size, unique optical properties, and good biocompatibility,offering broad application prospects in agriculture and related fields (Bhati et al., 2018; Dubey et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022a, 2022b). CDs application in soil regulated beneficial microbial communities (e.g., Aspergillus, Fusobacterium, Gemmatimonadetes and Actinomycetes), thereby enhancing nitrogen release from the soil (Wang et al., 2022a, 2022b). CDs also stimulated root secretion (e.g., amino acids, organic acids, and auxins) and recruit beneficial microorganisms (e.g. Actinobacteria, Ascomycetes, Acidobacteria and Mycobacteria) to promote nitrogen activation in soil (Ji et al., 2023).

China is the largest producer of sweetpotato, with a cultivated area of 2.206 million hectares (Fao, 2021). As a vital food and economic crop, sweetpotato plays a critical role in ensuring food security and promoting economic development through its dual functions as a stable food and starch production functions. Sweetpotato planting affects soil microbial communities (Wang et al., 2023a, 2023b), however, it is not clear whether this affects the ability to increase microbial residual carbon accumulation to increase soil organic carbon. Although numerous studies have explored the effects of CDs on crop growth and soil microbial communities, the mechanisms by which crop-CDs interactions regulate accumulation of microbial necromass C have not been elucidated. Therefore, it is of great significance to explore the effects of CDs, sweetpotato and their interaction on concentrations of microbial necromass C for clarifying their contribution to sequestration of organic carbon pools. We hypothesized that 1) significant differences in microbial necromass C and their contributions to soil organic carbon would occur through CDs application and sweetpotato planting. 2) The significant differences could be relevant to bacterial and fungal community structure and diversity. Based on these assumptions, we designed experiments to understand the concentration of microbial necromass and the corresponding controlling factors, which had important implications for evaluating the contribution of microbial-derived C to soil carbon pools under CDs application and sweetpotato planting. The findings will advance strategies for enhancing soil organic carbon sequestration, offering solutions to mitigate global climate change and improve agricultural management practices.

The soils collected from sweetpotato cropping field (117°37′ E, 34°49′ N) in Jiawang District, Xuzhou City, Jiangsu Province, China. The soil type is Vertisol and properties (0–20 cm) are given as followings: The soil organic carbon (SOC) concentration 16.02 g kg−1, pH 6.45, soil bulk density (BD) 1.54 g cm−3, total nitrogen (TN) 1.68 g kg−1, available potassium (AK) 102.7 mg kg−1, dissolved organic carbon (DOC) 187 mg kg−1. Soil was sampled, air-dried, and sieved through a 2 mm mesh and mixed well for pot experiments.

CDs were derived from biomass of S. miltiorrhiza and were provided by Jiangsu Normal University. The method details of CDs synthesis and characterization were referred to previous research (Li et al., 2022). DOC concentration and pH of CDs were 11,414 mg kg−1 and 5.46.

Soil was weighed (1.8 kg dry mass) into each pot and preincubated for 24 h to stabilize the microbial activity. After preincubation, the soil in the pots was replenished with deionized water to adjust the moisture to 80 % of its water-holding capacity. Four treatments were set up for this experiment: soil amended with deionized-water (CK); soil with CDs application (CDs, 1.5 mg mL−1, 100 mL); soil planted with sweetpotato (CK-SP); and soil with both CDs application (1.5 mg mL−1, 100 mL) and sweetpotato planting (CDs-SP). Each treatment was set up four replications. Healthy cuttings (15 cm in length) of sweetpotato cultivars (Xushu 32) were transplanted into designated pots (CK-SP and CDs-SP treatments). After one week for the survival of sweetpotato seedlings, CDs were added into designated pots (CDs and CDs-SP treatments), while an equivalent volume of deionized water was added to the control treatments (CK and CK-SP). The pot experiment lasted for 42 days, and 6 times CDs were applied for the entire experimental process. One week after transplanting sweetpotato seedlings was the slowing down period. Next, carbon dots were applied every five days, and the treatments without carbon dots were replaced with an equal amount of deionized water. Since soil microorganisms and their necromass have a rapid formation cycle, we only performed short-term incubations.

After potting completion, shoots and roots were cut from sweetpotato for weighting fresh and dry mass. Soils were sampled to sieve and remove roots or other impurities. These treated soil samples were divided into three parts. One part was air-dried for the determination of soil physicochemical properties and enzyme activities. The other part was stored at 4 °C for DOC and its structure deteminations. The remaining soil was stored at −80 °C for DNA extraction.

The SOC concentration was determined by H2SO4-K2Cr2O7 oxidation-reduction method (Walkley and Black, 1934) and DOC was extracted from soil with ultrapure water for 1 h, followed by passing through a 0.45 μm filter, and analyzed by a TOC element analyzer (Analytikjena, multiN/C 3100, Germany). Soil DOC was extracted for analysis of functional groups. Soil DOC extract was diluted to 20 mg L−1 to avoid the influence of concentration on UV absorbance. Samples were measured with a UV–vis spectrometer using a quartz cell. Absorbance values at 210 nm (A210 nm) and 280 nm (A280 nm) were selected to characterize amines and aromatic compounds in solution (Kalbitz et al., 2003), respectively. A ratio of absorbance at 250 nm–365 nm (A2/A3) represented averaged molecular weight of DOC when the smaller the ratio, the greater the molecular weight (Song et al., 2020).

Soil extracellular enzyme activities associated with C-acquiring [β-1,4-glucosidase (SG), β-α-cellobiohydrolase (CBH), β-1,4-xylosidase (βX) and phenol oxidase (POX)] were determined according to reagent kit manual (Bell et al., 2013; Luo et al., 2018). Using 7-amino-4 methylcoumarin and 4-methylumbelliferone as fluorescent substrates, and standard fluorometric technique was used to measure the activities of the SG and CBH. Using nitrophenol and pyrocatechol as fluorescent substrates, and standard fluorometric technique was used to measure the activities of the βX and POX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial and fungal necromass C measurement and calculation
ce:section.ce:para:
Amino sugars included glucosamine (GluN), galactosamine (GalN), mannosamine (ManN), and muramic acid (MurA) were analyzed according to the methods (Amelung et al., 2008; Shao et al., 2017) by gas chromatography. Briefly, soil samples (1.0 g) were weighed into tubes and then hydrolyzed with HCl (6 mol L−1, 10 mL) at 105 °C for 8 h. After the hydrolysis products were cooled, 100 μL of the internal standard inositol (1 mg mL−1) was added and filtered through qualitative filter papers. The filtrate was completely dried using a rotary evaporator at 65 °C and redissolved in deionized water (20 mL). The pH of the samples was adjusted to 6.6–6.8 and the samples were centrifuged (4000 rpm, 10 min) and freeze-dried. The extracted amino sugar was dissolved in 1 mL of deionized water, then 100 μL of the internal standard N-methyl-D-glucosamine (1 mg mL−1) was added, mixed and freeze-dried. Standard sample preparation: 100 μL mixed standards (glucosamine, galactosamine, mannosamine and muramic acid) were dried and then added sequentially to inositol, N-methyl-D-glucosamine, and 1 mL deionized water. The purified samples and standard series of the amino sugars were reacted with hydroxylamine hydrochloride and 4-(dimethylamino) pyridine for 35 min at 80 °C, and the vials were shaken 5–7 times using a vortex apparatus. The vial was cooled to room temperature, 1 mL of acetic anhydride was added. The reaction was carried out at 80 °C for 25 min, and the vial was shaken 5 times using a vortex apparatus during heating process. After cooling, the resulting amino sugars was extracted with 1.5 mL dichloromethane and shaken for 30 s to mix thoroughly. Then, excess acetic anhydride was removed with 1 mL (1 mol L−1 HCl) and deionized water (3 times). The final organic phase was dried with N2 at 45 °C, and the residue was dissolved in 200 μL of ethyl acetate-hexane (1:1). The amino sugar derivatives were separated on a gas chromatograph equipped with a VT-1 column. The temperature program was set as follows: temperature held at 120 °C, increased at a rate of 10 °C per min to 200 °C, and increased at 5 °C per min to 250 °C. The gas chromatography inlet was set to 300 °C. GluN and MurA were used for calculating fungal and bacterial necromass based on Eqs. (1) and (2), according to the methods of Appuhn and Joergensen (2006) and Liang et al. (2019).
(1)
Fungal necromass
C
FNC
=
GluN / 179.17 − 2 × MurA / 251.23
× 179.17 × 9
(2)
Bacterial necromass
C
BNC
= MurA × 45

In detail, 179.17 and 251.23 in Eq. (1) are the molecular weights of GluN and MurA, respectively, where 9 is the conversion value of GluN to fungal necromass C, and 45 is the conversion factor from MurA to bacterial necromass C (Appuhn and Joergensen, 2006; Joergensen, 2018). The proportion of total microbial necromass C in SOC represents the microbial necromass contribution to SOC sequestration (Liang et al., 2019).

Soil total DNA was extracted with a Power Soil TM kit according to the manufacturer's instructions. The quality and concentration of the extracted DNA were determined using a NanoDrop spectrophotometer (NanoDrop, Wilmington, DE, USA). Before sequencing, PCR reactions were performed with 2 μL DNA for bacteria (16S rRNA gene) and 1 μL for fungi (ITS gene region). The bacteria and fungi primers were 338 F: 5’-ACTCCTACGGGAGGCAGCA-3, 806 R: 5’-GGACTACNNGGGTATCTAAT-3’ and ITS1 F: 5’-CTTGGTCATTTAGAGGAAGTAA-3’and ITS1 R: 5’-GCTGCGTTCTTCATCGATGC-3’. And the PCR reactions were performed according to the previous study (Parvizi and Ready, 2008).

According to the standard protocol, the Illumina NovaSeq platform was used to analyze bacterial and fungal communities. After sequencing, samples were differentiated based on the barcodes and primers of the sequences, and sequence orientation was adjusted. The paired reads were concatenated into a sequence basing on the overlap relationship between PE reads using Pandaseq (Masella et al., 2012). Sequences with a tail mass of <20 reads and an N length of 5 % of the total sequence length were filtered out using PRINSEQ (Schmieder and Edwards, 2011). The DADA2 method were utilized for primer removal, quality filtering and denoising. Taxonomy was assigned to ASVs by comparing the reads with the Silva database using a Naive Bayes classifier (Quast et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2007). Based on the abundance and annotation information of ASV, the proportions of sequence at different classification levels for samples were statistically analyzed to evaluate the resolution and complexity of species annotation.

The Tukey's multiple test with IBM SPSS 25 software was used to analyze the significance of differences at P < 0.05 level among treatments. Sigmaplot software was performed to evaluate the influence of different treatments on weights of sweetpotato organs, soil carbon concentrations, enzyme activities and structure characteristics of microbial community. The Bray-Curtis dissimilarity metric and non-metric multidimensional scaling (NMDS) were used to investigate the effects of different treatments on soil microbial community structure. Linear discriminant analysis (LDA) was used to examine the discriminating microbial genera among treatments (P < 0.05). Co- occurrence networks (CoNet) were based on 16 samples between CDs application (CDs and CDs-SP treatments) and sweetpotato planting (CK-SP and CDs-SP treatment), The correlations within the relative abundance of ASVs network were calculated for each treatment, using “igraph”, “Hmisc”, “ggplot2”, “ggpubr”, “ggsignif” packages with Spearman correlation under R, and only strong relationships (Spearman's r > 0.8, P < 0.05) were chosen for the construction of networks. The networks were visualized, and topological parameters were calculated using Gephi software (version 0.9.2). Structural equation model (SEM) analysis was applied to test for relationships among soil properties and the characteristics of bacterial communities using AMOS software (IBM SPSS AMOS 22). The parameters for testing the overall goodness of model fitting are non-significant chi-square (χ2) test (P > 0.05), high comparative fit index (GFI > 0.8), and low root mean square error of approximation (RMSEA < 0.05). The pearson correlation heatmaps were calculated and plotted using the R “coreplot” package.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Weight of sweetpotato organs and soil concentrations of SOC and DOC
ce:section.ce:para: Soil application of CDs showed the promoting effect on sweetpotato growth compared to soil without CDs treatment. Compared to CK-SP treatment, CDs-SP treatment increased the fresh weights of shoots and roots by 24.7 % and 57.9 % (Fig. S1), and their dry weights by 29.1 % and 24.6 %, respectively. Compared to CK treatment, soil application CDs and planting sweetpotato significantly improved SOC content (P < 0.05), which reached the highest under CDs-SP treatment (Fig. 1 ). Meanwhile, significant increases in soil DOC concentration by 99.7 % and 51.4 % were detected under CDs and CDs-SP treatments, while it was no significant difference between CK and CK-SP treatment.

Statistically significant differences in A210nm value (CO-NH functional group, amines) were not detected (P > 0.05) among CK, CDs and CK-SP, but CDs-SP treatment decreased A210nm value by 57.3 %, 61.9 % and 51.0 %, respectively. Unlike A210nm, CDs and CDs-SP treatments greatly increased absorbance value of soil DOC solution at A280 nm (aromatic compounds) by 343 % and 150 % compared to CK treatment, respectively. Planting sweetpotato separately, there is no significant difference of A280nm value in comparison to CK treatment. Only an elevating A2/A3 value was determined in CK-SP treatment (Fig. 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Soil enzyme activity related to carbon cycling and basic soil properties
ce:section.ce:para: Compared to CK treatment, CDs and CK-SP treatments significantly increased the β-1,4-glucosidase by 38.8 %, 23.5 %, and increased phenol oxidase activities by 30.3 % and 9.9 %, respectively. While a significantly increase for β-1,4-glucosidase and a significanly decrease for phenol oxidase under CDs-SP treatment compared with CK treatment. Greatly decreases in β-α-cellobiohydrolase and β-1,4-xylosidase were observed under CK-SP treatment comparing with CDs-SP (Fig. 2 ). SP planting significantly decreased the soil moisture content but increased pH (Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Soil amino sugar biomarkers, microbial necromass C and its contributions to SOC
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-SP treatment exhibited higher soil glucosamine and galactosamine concentrations than CK treatment, while CDs and CK-SP treatments had lower galactosamine concentrations (Fig. S3). Soil CDs application had a significantly increase for mannosamine concentration, but a significantly decrease of mannosamine was detected under CK-SP treatment compared to CK treatment. Whether CDs application, planting sweetpotato separately or their interaction, they all significantly decreased the concentrations of soil muramic acid.

Opposite trends were observed between bacterial and fungal necromass C concentrations under different treatments. Compared to CK treatment, greatly decreases for bacterial necromass C and increases for fungal necromass C were observed under CDs, CK-SP and CDs-SP treatments. The reductions were 14 %, 25 % and 34 %, the increase in volume were 17 %, 22 % and 48 %. There were similar trends for contributions of bacteria and fungal to SOC as bacterial and fungal necromass C, respectively (Fig. 3 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Soil microbial community structure and α-diversity
ce:section.ce:para: Soil microbial β-diversity inhibited their community structure. Soil application of CDs (CDs and CDs-SP) showed differences from soil without CDs (CK and CK-SP) in structure of bacterial community along NMDS2, whereas the structure of bacterial community in soils with cultivitating sweetpotato (CDs-SP and CK-SP) was similar along NMDS1. For the structure of fungal community, CDs-SP treatment was greatly different from other treatments along NMDS1 (Fig. 4 ).

CDs treatment significantly increased the bacterial richness (Chao 1 index), while a significant decrease was observed in bacterial diversity (Shannon index) compared with CK treatment. Significant decreases in fungal Chao1 and Shannon indices were detected under CDs, CK-SP and CDs-SP treatments compared with CK treatment (Fig. 4).

For bacterial communities, the predominant phyla were Proteobacteria (29.4 % ∼ 40.0 %), Acidobacteria (14.8 % ∼ 19.1 %), Actinobacteria (7.5 % ∼ 12.5 %), Gemmatimonadota (7.2 % ∼ 11.2 %), Chloroflexi (7.1 % ∼ 8.2 %) and Patescibacteria (4.6 % ∼ 5.5 %). In addition, seven phyla (Planctomycetota, Firmicutes, Verrucomicrobiota, Myxococcota, Bacteroidota, Cyanobacteria and Latescibacterota) were found in soil among all the treatments (Fig. 5A). The relative abundances of Planctomycetota, Verrucomicrobiota, Myxococcota, and Bacteroidota were <5 % but >1 %. The ten main genera (Sphingomonas, Gemmatimonas, Bacillus, RB41, SC-I-84, Vicinamibacteraceae, Ramlibacter, TK10, MND1 and Haliangium) were found in the soils among all the treatments. The result showed that the relative abundance of genus Bacillus significantly increased and Gemmatimonas and Vicinamibacteraceae decreased for CDs and CDs-SP treatments compared to the non application of CDs. Planting sweetpotato (CK-SP) greatly improved the relative abundance of Sphingomonas, while it significantly decreased the relative abundances of Ramlibacter and MND1 compared with CK treatment (Fig. 5B).

For fungal communities, the Ascomycota and Basidiomycota were the dominant phyla, with relative abundances of 63 % ∼ 84 % and 5 % ∼ 13 %, respectively (Fig. 5C). The Penicillium, Fusarium and Meyerozyma were the predominant genera, with the relative abundances of 5 % ∼ 36 %, 2 % ∼ 25 % and 0.1 % ∼ 18 %, respectively (Fig. 5D). For planting sweetpotato, the relative abundances for Meyerozyma, Solicoccozyma and Arxotrichum were significantly higher in CDs-SP treatment than that in CK-SP treatment. Compared to CDs treatment, significantly increases for the relative abundance of Fusarium, Meyerozyma, Solicoccozyma, Humicola and Arxotrichum were detected in CDs-SP treatment.

For the treatments of soil non-application of CDs, planting sweetpotato had a different effect on Latescibacterota, Armatimonadota and WPS-2 at phylum level in heatmap (Fig. S4). However, for soil application of CDs, the obviously differences in the Actinobacteriota, Bdellovibrionota, Entotheonellaeota, Deinococcota and WPS-2 were found between samples of CDs and CDs-SP treatments. We further tested the effects of CDs and sweetpotato on the bacterial and fungal genera using the linear discriminant analysis (LDA, Fig. 6 ). The results revealed that the relative abundances of certain bacterial genera were significantly shifted by planting sweetpotato without application of CDs. And Burkholderia_Caballeronia_Paraburkholderia, Acinetobacter, Pseudarthrobacter, Roseisolibacter, Dongia, Pajaroellobacter, Bradyrhizobium, Ralstonia, Allorhizobium_Neorhizobium_Pararhizobium were detected by planting sweetpotato with application of CDs (LDA ≥ 2, P < 0.05). We found significant decreases for fungal genera in CDs application soil without planting sweetpotato, and soil with CDs application, certain fungal genera were shifted by planting sweetpotato.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relationships of soil physicochemical properties, enzyme activities, bacterial and fungal communities as well as microbial necromass C
ce:section.ce:para: Spearman's correlation analysis indicated that pH, SG displayed positive relationships with FNC and FNC/SOC (Fig. 7 ). SM, A210 nm, BNC, BNC/SOC showed negative relationships with FNC and FNC/SOC. On the contrary, significantly positive relationships were showed between pH, SG and BNC, BNC/SOC, and negative correlations were revealed between SM, A210nm and BNC, BNC/SOC. Further, we analyzed the relationships between microbial necromass carbon and community structure. The results showed that FNC and FNC/SOC were positively regulated by structure of fungal community. Interestingly, BNC and BNC/SOC were significantly negatively relevance to fungal community structure. Furthermore, there was an extremely significantly negative correlation between fungal α-diversity and FNC as well as FNC/SOC (P < 0.001), which indicated the higher fungal taxa diversity, the lower the contribution of necromass carbon and their transformation to SOC. We analyzed the relationship between the abundances of dominant genera and microbial necromass carbon. The greatly positive relationship was analyzed between MND1 and BNC as well as BNC/SOC, while Bacillus had negative links with bacterial necromass C and contribution to SOC. The converse trends had been found in relationships of MND1, Bacillus, and FNC, FNC/SOC. For fungi, we found that a certain number of genera were related to mirobial necromass C. The positively relationships were found in the relative abundances of fungal genera (Penicillium and Rozellomycota) and bacterial necromass C, and the relative abundances of fungal genera (Humicola, Meyerozyma and Solicoccozyma) were positive with FNC and FNC/S

Based on the SEM (Fig. 8 ), the bacterial necromass C was directly controlled by bacterial community structure and β-1,4-glucosidase (SG), wherein a positively significant effect (P < 0.05) was analyzed between bacterial community structure and necromass C. On the contrary, there is a negatively significant effect was revealed between SG and bacterial necromass C. Soil DOC and DOC-A210nm had an importantly indirect effect on bacterial necromass C by influencing community structure and α-diversity of bacteria. We found that the fungal community structure and α-diversity directly impacted on fungal necromass C accumulation. And a positive relationship was established between fungal community structure and necromass C, conversely a negative correlation was found between α-diversity and necromass C. Wherein soil pH and SW were the mainly indirect factors to regulate necromass C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microbial co-occurrence network analysis under CDs application and cultivating sweetpotato
ce:section.ce:para: In general, the co-occurrence network patterns of core microbiome varied greatly among different treatments. We built a correlation network to reveal the co-occurrence among bacterial and fungal community composition under CDs application and planting sweetpotato (Fig. S5). Based on bacterial network analysis, we determined that topology network exhibited higher complexity and connectivity under CDs application treatments (CDs and CDs-SP), as evidenced by having a higher number of edges (6548), average degree (avgK: 15.572) and average clustering coefficient (avgCC: 0.551), while a higher number of an average path distance (APD: 6.04) and modularity (0.719) were displayed under cultivating sweetpotato treatments (CK-SP and CDs-SP) (Table S1). According to the ASVs of nodes in network, we can see the different modularity class between CDs application and cultivating sweetpotato treatments. Based on fungal network analysis, it was more simpler compared to bacterial networks. Compared cultivating sweetpotato treatments CDs application treatments exhibited higher complexity and connectivity of the fungal network.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of CDs application and sweetpotato planting on soil organic carbon and microbial necromass C in Vertisol
ce:section.ce:para: Vertisol is typically characterized by poor soil structure and low organic matter content, yet it exhibits high potential for productivity enhancement. Clarifying the positive effects of CDs and sweet potato cultivation on soil organic carbon content in Vertisol is not only crucial for improving the economic efficiency of sweet potato production, but also essential for the utilization of waste resources. The application of nanomaterials in agriculture offers considerable advantages, notably, our findings indicate that CDs application combined with sweetpotato planting increased SOC contents. This enhancement can be attributed to the carbon-rich properties of CDs, which improve soil fertility, organic matter and microbial biomass (Mahmoud et al., 2024). Wang et al. (2023a, 2023b) have reported that differences in SOC content between two types of sweetpotato rootsaccelerate carbon turnover by altering the activity of carbon cycle enzymes. Microbial necromass carbon constitutes a vital component of persistent SOC. Interestingly, ‌CDs, CK-SP, and CDs-SP treatments significantly reduced bacterial necromass carbon‌, whereas ‌fungal necromass carbon markedly increased under CDs and CK-SP treatments‌. This suggests that ‌fungal necromass accumulation drives SOC storage. The divergent effects on bacterial and fungal necromass C could be related to microbial life history strategies (Shao et al., 2021). Fungi preferentially utilize recalcitrant C substrates, whereas bacteria favor metabolically accessible, structurally simple C. Such differences ofin life history strategy drive changes in fungal and bacterial community diversity and structure (Yang et al., 2022). In this study, the organic carbon formed by both CDs and sweetpotato roots exhibits structural stability, which enhances its availability for fungal utilization, turnover and necromass carbon formation. Previous studies have demonstrated that organic inputs together with mineral fertilizers increase the microbial C sequestration potential in paddy soils (Chen et al., 2020). Agricultural practices involving continuous organic C inputs have shown to significantly increase the amount of microbial necromass in soils, but the fungal-derived necromass contributing substantially more to SOC than the bacterial-derived necromass (Li et al., 2015).

Spearman's correlation analysis indicated that the decrease of bacterial necromass concentration promoted the accumulation of fungal necromass, as well as an increase in contribution of fungal necromass C to SOC. These divergent patterns could be attributed to variations in soil types and experimental conditions. Specifically, straw amendment preferentially increased fungal necromass and its contribution to SOC, whereas manure application stimulated bacterial necromass production to a greater extent, thereby decreasing the ratio of fungal to bacterial necromass (Zhou et al., 2023). These findings ‌demonstrate‌ that exogenous amendments ‌exert selective effects‌ on bacterial and fungal necromass dynamics. The inconsistent accumulation ways of bacterial and fungal necromass in cropland soils under different treatments indicated their divergent accumulations mechanisms. We found the results with structural equation model that bacterial necromass C mainly depends on bacterial community structure and enzyme activity. The increased β-1,4-glucosidase activity resulted in low microbial carbon pump efficacy to stabilize C, limiting microorganisms from investing in growth and microbial necromass production (Yang et al., 2020) and resulting in less bacterial necromass C accumulation (Chi et al., 2019; Wilpiszeski et al., 2019). A higher SG activity would promote soil organic carbon turnover, which is beneficial for plants or microorganisms to use carbon sources to store their own energy, not form necromass. The shifts in bacterial community structure are mainly regulated by amine compounds in DOC. In our study materials, CDs contain a higher amount of DOC, which leads to the changes of bacterial community structure, not α-diversity. Microbial necromass represent a time-integrated indicator of microbial community growth and biomass turnover, thus the change in the composition of microbial necromass is significantly influenced by the changes in microbial community structure (Liang et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of CDs application and sweetpotato planting on soil microbial community structure
ce:section.ce:para: CDs treatment significantly improved the Chao 1 index of soil bacterial diversity, consistent with previous findings that nano-carbon fertilizers enhance bacterial community diversity in agricultural fields. (Han et al., 2021). The combined use of nano-fertilizers and conventional fertilizers maintain microbial diversity in wheat–maize rotation (Upadhyay et al., 2024). The combined utilization of nanofertilizers and compound fertilizers led to an augmentation in the diversity of the soil microbial community (Chen et al., 2023a, 2023b). However, the Chao 1 index and Shannon index of fungi were declined under application of CDs and planting of SP treatments in this research. This result promotes the formation of fungal necromass C.

In present study, Proteobacteria, Acidobacteria, Actinobacteria and Gemmatimonadota exhibited higher relative abundances at the phylum level across treatments, suggesting their critical roles in SOC turnover. Previous results showed that most of the microbial necromass at the operational taxonomic unit level (88.51 %), which mainly belong to Acidobacteria, Actinobacteria, Gemmatimonadetes, and Proteobacteria decomposed significantly after 30 days (Dong et al., 2021). The dominant bacterial phyla were Actinobacteria and Proteobacteria, which is chitinase-encoding gene harboring bacterial taxa (Zhang et al., 2022). Bacterial necromass C was significantly negatively correlated with cbbM-harboring bacterial diversity but positively correlated with cbbM-harboring bacterial community composition (Li et al., 2024).

In our study, the Sphingomonas and Gemmatimonas were the higher abundant genus of bacteria, they promoted the conversion of soil organic carbon and available nitrogen and phosphorus (Wang et al., 2022a, 2022b). Liu et al. (2021) reported that the proportion of Sphingomonas and Gemmatimonas were related to the metabolism and transformation of nitrogen. Furthermore, Sphingomonas and Gemmatimonas are the potential keystone genus and their specific functions significantly influenced the priming effect (Zhang et al., 2024). Nano calcium carbonate mixed with compound fertilizer elevated the abundance of Actinobacteria, Acidobacteria, Chloroflexi, Cyanobacteria, and Nitrospirae (Gao et al., 2024). Regarding the bacterial community after application of nano-fertlizers, the relative abundances of Acidobacteria, Chthonomonadetes, and Saccharimonadia decreased, whereas Acidimicrobiia, Chloroflexia, and Gemmatimonadetes increased with nano-fertlizers application (Pide et al., 2023). Although Latescibacterota had low abundance, the difference has been found following planting sweetpotato. We found that Ascomycota and Basidiomycota were the most abundant fungal genus phyla, and they were the key groups forming microbial necromass and SOC (Meng et al., 2024). Nanoparticles can significantly affect the soil microbial community (Shah et al., 2022). The complexity and modularity of the soil bacterial community were different between CDs application and SP planting. The co-occurrence networks analysis was used to explore the effect of different treatment on network complexity and the keystone microbiome. This implied that CDs application can mediate the relationships among bacterial ASVs and had more complexity. Changes in bacterial community structure altered their elemental stoichiometry and energy requirements as well as nutrient acquisition strategies, which determine the formation of bacterial necromass (Čapek et al., 2021). And the fungal necromass accumulations depended on α-diversity and community structure of fungi. Declining fungal diversity implies an increase in the abundance of specific fungal taxa, and the high microbial abundance promotes microbial necromass formation through sequential assimilation, synthesis, and turnover of carbon inputs (Jia et al., 2023).

Our study demonstrated that both CDs application and sweetpotato planting predominantly promoted fungal necromass C fixation over bacterial contributions. NMDS revealed that soil bacterial community structure was mainly altered by CDs application, while the fungal community was co-regulated by the interaction of CDs and sweetpotato. CDs significantly increased the Chao 1 index but decreased the Shannon index. Both individual and combined treatments significantly diminished fungal α-diversity index. Proteobacteria, Acidobacteria, Actinobacteria, Gemmatimonadota, Chloroflexi and Patescibacteria were the dominant bacterial phyla. The fungal phyla with higher relative abundance were Ascomycota and Basidiomycota. Our study further revealed that the accumulation of bacterial necromass C is directly regulated by their community structure and activity of carbon cycle enzymes, and the accumulation of fungal necromass C is mainly a result of changes in community structure and diversity of fungi. In summary, this study elucidated the differential effects of CDs application and sweetpotato planting to promote carbon sequestration by soil microbial necromass, which provides a theoretical basis for the application of nanomaterials to promote organic carbon sequestration in sweetpotato field.

Jing Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Meng Zhu: Investigation. Xiaoya Zhu: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Qiangqiang Zhang: Investigation. Yongchao Yu: Software, Methodology. Peng Zhao: Visualization, Supervision. Ming Liu: Writing – review & editing, Software. Rong Jin: Visualization, Supervision. Zhonghou Tang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
